# flowfig â Architecture Plan Two producers, one translation bridge, one writer. Existing pages get **captured** by a Chrome extension. Generated code gets **compiled** by an installable CLI. Both land on the same file format, and only one module â the Figma plugin's Node Writer â is ever allowed to touch `figma.*`. Same branding family as flowkit â **no functional relationship** â but the same DNA: one repo, several independently-shipped surfaces, a shared core underneath, and a spec that teaches AI agents the local conventions. --- ## Pipeline overview ``` Producer A â existing pages Producer B â generated code âââââââââââââââââââââââââââââ âââââââââââââââââââââââââââââ â Chrome extension â â agent-kit CLI â â real live DOM â â â Playwright-rendered â â â getComputedStyle(), â â real layout engine, no â â getBoundingClientRect() â â browser tab needed â âââââââââââââââ¬âââââââââââââââ âââââââââââââââ¬âââââââââââââââ â resolve() â resolve() + translate() â¼ â¼ âââââââââââââââââââââââââââââ âââââââââââââââââââââââââââââ â *.capture.flowfig.json â â *.translated.flowfig.json â â resolved, untranslated â â â tagged refs already â â arbitrary CSS, no tag â â resolved to component/ â â guarantees â â token keys â âââââââââââââââ¬âââââââââââââââ âââââââââââââââ¬âââââââââââââââ âââââââââââââââââââââ¬ââââââââââââââââââââââââââââ â¼ ââââââââââââââââââââââââââââââââââââââââ â Figma plugin â the only figma.* caller â â â¢ Import â accepts either file kind â â â¢ Scrape â components/variables/ â â styles â symbol table â â â¢ Node Writer â key-resolve or fuzzy- â â match, then write â ââââââââââââââââââââââââââââââââââââââââ â â¤´ exports .flowfig/symbols.json, read back by agent-kit for tagging â the mirror runs in both directions ``` --- ## The three file kinds Everything in this system moves as a file hand-off, never a live connection â consistent end to end, from capture to symbol-table export. | File | Produced by | Contains | Consumed by | |---|---|---|---| | `*.capture.flowfig.json` | extension (`resolve()` only) | Resolved-but-untranslated tree â raw arbitrary CSS | plugin: full translate + fuzzy-match + write | | `*.translated.flowfig.json` | agent-kit (`resolve()` + `translate()`) | Already-translated node spec, with tagged component/token refs | plugin: key-resolution + write only | | `.flowfig/symbols.json` | plugin's scraper | Symbol table â components/variables/styles scraped live | agent-kit + the agent itself, read off disk | --- ## Tagging convention The agent has the symbol table on disk before it generates anything, so it can reference real Figma objects by their stable key â not a name that can drift. ```html
/* token-level reference â survives cascade because the CLI reads declared CSS source, not just computed output */ .price { background: var(--ffig-color-accent-500); } ``` Untagged elements and values still work â they just fall back to the same fuzzy-matching parity engine the extension's arbitrary captures rely on. Tagging isn't all-or-nothing per file. --- ## Node Writer â two resolution modes, side by side - **Key resolution** (tagged) â deterministic. Look up the key live via the Plugin API and bind/instantiate. No ambiguity. - **Fuzzy matching** (untagged) â exact match binds a Variable/Style; close match surfaces as a suggestion; no match is a per-run user choice (literal value or mint a new token). Three outcomes when a tagged `componentRef` is hit: 1. **Resolves in the open file** â bind/instantiate. Happy path. 2. **Known key, missing from this file** â surface to the user rather than guess. Also how a stale symbol-table export gets caught â the Writer always re-verifies live, so staleness only ever bites the agent's tags, never the write itself. 3. **Explicitly flagged as new** â Writer creates the component from the translated subtree, registers it in the file, and **writes the fresh key back into the symbol table**. The mirror runs both directions: Figma â code by scrape, code â Figma â code by this write-back. --- ## Publish matrix Four workspace packages. Only one of them ever runs `npm publish` â the other three ship somewhere else entirely, or nowhere. | Package | Ships via | Notes | |---|---|---| | `agent-kit` | **npm** | The flagship, installable CLI. Any project can add it and generate Figma files straight from its own code. | | `figma-plugin` | Figma Community | Reviewed and distributed by Figma, not npm. Importer, scraper, Node Writer. | | `extension` | Chrome Web Store | Reviewed and distributed by Google, not npm. Live-page capture only. | | `core` | *private* | Never published, never leaves the monorepo. Exists only for clean cross-bundler imports. | --- ## Repo layout ``` flowfig/ âââ package.json # npm workspaces: "packages/*" âââ tsconfig.base.json âââ packages/ â âââ core/ # private â resolver, translator, design-system matching â âââ figma-plugin/ # manifest.json (Figma's) + main/ui + scraper + Node Writer â âââ extension/ # manifest.json (Chrome MV3) + content script + popup â âââ agent-kit/ # the one npm-published package â CLI + tagging + agent guide âââ examples/ # shared fixtures â keeps the two adapters honest against one contract âââ docs/ # ships to consumers â incl. agent-kit's generated AGENT-GUIDE.md âââ Documentation/ # dev-only â architecture rationale, decisions log ``` --- ## CSS resolution â why the two producers differ The extension never needs a cascade engine of its own â it's already running inside a real, rendered tab, so `getComputedStyle()`/`getBoundingClientRect()` give it ground truth for free. It only needs `core/resolver`'s normalization step and `core/format`; it never even bundles `core/translator`, since capture files are translated later, inside the plugin. agent-kit has no browser at all, so it depends on a real one: **Playwright, as a hard dependency from day one** â chosen over a lighter static-parse-first fallback chain for simplicity, and because it's genuinely more capable (true layout for non-auto-layout content and intrinsic sizing that static CSS parsing or jsdom alone can't produce â jsdom resolves cascade but has no layout engine at all). --- ## MVP roadmap | Phase | Focus | Notes | |---|---|---| | P0 | Core engine skeleton | Resolver + Translator as pure, fixture-tested TS packages â no browser or Figma runtime needed to validate the schema. | | P1 | Figma plugin â writer + scraper | Node Writer proven against hand-built fixture files; scraper exports the first real symbol table. Nothing downstream is useful without this existing first. | | P2 | agent-kit CLI â the flagship | Playwright resolve+translate, tagging convention, published to npm. This is the product most users actually install. | | P3 | Chrome extension | Secondary capture producer, for arbitrary existing pages rather than agent-generated ones. | | P4 | Design-system parity depth | Variant-matching precision, fuzzy-match tuning, symbol-table sync UX. | | P5 | Fidelity polish | Vector-vs-raster handling, font fallback tables, gradients/shadows â effects. Multi-viewport/interactive-state capture is a deliberate boundary, not a queued phase. | --- ## Foreseeable issues â already designed around 1. **Schema/version skew.** agent-kit publishes to npm instantly; figma-plugin goes through Figma Community's review, which can lag days. Every file carries an explicit `formatVersion`; the plugin fails loudly and specifically on a mismatch rather than misparsing. 2. **Fonts, twice over.** `figma.loadFontAsync` only works with fonts the file actually has â needs a fallback-mapping table. Separately, Playwright needs the same webfont to lay out text correctly in the first place, which can fail quietly in a network-restricted agent sandbox. 3. **Playwright's footprint in agent sandboxes.** Install/launch friction is a real setup step for agent-kit's actual audience (CI-like, constrained environments) â documented explicitly in the agent guide, not assumed to "just work." 4. **Vector vs. raster.** Inline ``/background photographs â image fill. An explicit detection rule, not an accident of whatever's easiest. 5. **Scope boundary.** One fixed viewport, one DOM state, per generation. Hover/focus/transitions/breakpoints are out of scope for v1 â stated up front, not discovered later. --- ## Decisions still open 1. Snippet storage: plugin-local `clientStorage` only, or should snippets also sync somewhere (so one captured/generated on one machine is insertable from another)? 2. Fuzzy-match default, for the extension/capture path specifically (the tagged agent-kit path is already deterministic): auto-bind exact matches silently, or always surface a confirmation list? 3. Component-level detection for arbitrary captured pages â v1 scopes captures to value-level parity only (colors/spacing/type bound to tokens), with heuristic structural component-matching deferred rather than promised. Confirm that's the right v1 cut. 4. Format-version policy: hard-reject a `formatVersion` mismatch outright, or attempt best-effort backward migration for older files? 5. `@create-figma-plugin` framework adoption vs. a hand-rolled build for the plugin package. --- *flowfig Â· architecture plan Â· updated 2026-07-18 â not yet pushed, repo access pending*

content.text: Let's plan this properly and start after refining it

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

addedBlocks:
## claude.ai Figma
The official Figma MCP server. Use this server whenever the user wants to create, generate, edit, implement, or sync any design, UI, screen, component, mockup, or visual — in Figma, FigJam, Figma Make, or Figma Slides — and whenever the user mentions Figma or provides a figma.com URL.

This server bridges code and design in both directions, and supports designing from scratch using existing design systems and codebases.

CAPABILITIES:
- Read designs FROM Figma into code (get_design_context, get_screenshot, get_metadata, get_figjam)
- Write designs INTO Figma from code, intent, or existing components (use_figma, generate_figma_design, create_new_file, upload_assets)
- Bridge code and design via Code Connect (get_code_connect_map, add_code_connect_map)
- Create diagrams and FigJam content (generate_diagram, get_figjam)

WHEN TO USE THESE TOOLS:
- The user wants to create, mock up, or generate any UI, screen, component, or design — even if Figma isn't named
- The user wants to implement a design as code (design-to-code)
- The user wants to push a page, view, or component into Figma (code-to-design)
- The user wants to update, sync, or edit an existing Figma file
- The user shares a figma.com URL
- The user wants to build or extend a design system, design tokens, or component library
- The user wants to create a diagram in FigJam

SKILLS (Prefer the skills shipped with the Figma plugin on the agent being used. If no Figma plugin is installed, use the skills served by the Figma MCP resource):
- /figma-use — MANDATORY before calling use_figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-use/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-design — for translating an app page or layout into Figma; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-design/SKILL.md
- /figma-generate-library — for building a design system in Figma from code; fallback: skill://figma/figma-generate-library/SKILL.md
- /figma-code-connect — for mapping Figma components to codebase components; fallback: skill://figma/figma-code-connect/SKILL.md
- /figma-use-figjam — for FigJam-specific use_f… [truncated]

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact page can be granted — behavior static HTML cannot provide on its own, such as the page reading live or connected data, keeping state shared across viewers, or updating and republishing itself. Serves this user's live capability roster and the typed call definitions. Load it whenever the user asks for an artifact needing any such runtime behavior.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: The document isn't saved in the repo — the repo currently only has a README. Before I turn this into an execution plan, the plan itself flags five open decisions that are genuinely yours to make (they shape the architecture, not just implementation details). Let me get your calls on the four most consequential ones first.

questions.question: Snippet storage: should captured/generated snippets stay plugin-local, or sync across machines?

options.label: clientStorage only (Recommended)
options.description: No backend/auth to build or host. Matches the MVP's file-hand-off philosophy — nothing lives in a service. Snippet made on one machine isn't insertable from another.

options.label: Sync somewhere
options.description: Requires a hosted service + auth, which no other part of this architecture needs yet. Bigger scope, but enables cross-machine snippet reuse.

questions.question: formatVersion mismatch policy: hard-reject, or attempt best-effort backward migration?

options.label: Hard-reject (Recommended)
options.description: Simplest and safest — matches the 'fails loudly and specifically' behavior already described under Foreseeable Issues. No migration code to maintain from day one.

options.label: Best-effort migration
options.description: More resilient to Figma Community's review lag stranding users on an old plugin version, but adds migration-shim maintenance burden immediately.

questions.question: figma-plugin package: adopt @create-figma-plugin, or hand-roll the build?

options.label: @create-figma-plugin (Recommended)
options.description: Batteries-included bundler/HMR/UI scaffolding purpose-built for Figma plugins. Widely used, saves setup time for P1.

options.label: Hand-rolled build
options.description: Full control over bundler config with no framework opinions or dependencies, but you own all the plumbing yourself.

questions.question: Capture-path v1 scope: confirm value-level parity only (colors/spacing/type → tokens), deferring structural component-matching?

options.label: Confirm as scoped (Recommended)
options.description: Keeps P3 (Chrome extension) small and shippable. Structural/heuristic component-matching moves to a later phase, as the plan already implies.

options.label: Include structural detection in v1
options.description: Bigger P3 scope and longer time-to-first-ship for the extension, but captured pages get component-level fidelity sooner.

content.text: One more open decision remains: the fuzzy-match default behavior for the untagged capture path.

questions.question: For the capture path's fuzzy-matching, should exact matches to a Variable/Style auto-bind silently, or always surface a confirmation list to the user?

options.label: Auto-bind exact matches silently (Recommended)

input.content:
# flowfig — Architecture Plan Two producers, one translation bridge, one writer. Existing pages get **captured** by a Chrome extension. Generated code gets **compiled** by an installable CLI. Both land on the same file format, and only one module — the Figma plugin's Node Writer — is ever allowed to touch `figma.*`. Same branding family as flowkit — **no functional relationship** — but the same DNA: one repo, several independently-shipped surfaces, a shared core underneath, and a spec that teaches AI agents the local conventions. --- ## Pipeline overview ``` Producer A — existing pages Producer B — generated code ───────────────────────────── ───────────────────────────── │ Chrome extension │ │ agent-kit CLI │ │ real live DOM → │ │ Playwright-rendered → │ │ getComputedStyle(), │ │ real layout engine, no │ │ getBoundingClientRect() │ │ browser tab needed │ ──────────────┬────────────── ──────────────┬────────────── │ resolve() │ resolve() + translate() ▼ ▼ ───────────────────────────── ───────────────────────────── │ *.capture.flowfig.json │ │ *.translated.flowfig.json │ │ resolved, untranslated → │ │ tagged refs already │ │ arbitrary CSS, no tag │ │ resolved to component/ │ │ guarantees │ │ token keys │ ──────────────┬────────────── ──────────────┬────────────── └─────────────────────┬──────────────────────────┘ ▼ ──────────────────────────────────────── │ Figma plugin — the only figma.* caller │ │ • Import — accepts either file kind │ │ • Scrape — components/variables/ │ │ styles → symbol table │ │ • Node Writer — key-resolve or fuzzy- │ │ match, then write │ ──────────────────────────────────────── │ └─▶ exports .flowfig/symbols.json, read back by agent-kit for tagging — the mirror runs in both directions ``` --- ## The three file kinds Everything in this system moves as a file hand-off, never a live connection — consistent end to end, from capture to symbol-table export. | File | Produced by | Contains | Consumed by | |---|---|---|---| | `*.capture.flowfig.json` | extension (`resolve()` only) | Resolved-but-untranslated tree — raw arbitrary CSS | plugin: full translate + fuzzy-match + write | | `*.translated.flowfig.json` | agent-kit (`resolve()` + `translate()`) | Already-translated node spec, with tagged component/token refs | plugin: key-resolution + write only | | `.flowfig/symbols.json` | plugin's scraper | Symbol table — components/variables/styles scraped live | agent-kit + the agent itself, read off disk | --- ## Tagging convention The agent has the symbol table on disk before it generates anything, so it can reference real Figma objects by their stable key — not a name that can drift. ```html
/* token-level reference — survives cascade because the CLI reads declared CSS source, not just computed output */ .price { background: var(--ffig-color-accent-500); } ``` Untagged elements and values still work — they just fall back to the same fuzzy-matching parity engine the extension's arbitrary captures rely on. Tagging isn't all-or-nothing per file. --- ## Node Writer — two resolution modes, side by side - **Key resolution** (tagged) — deterministic. Look up the key live via the Plugin API and bind/instantiate. No ambiguity. - **Fuzzy matching** (untagged) — exact match binds a Variable/Style; close match surfaces as a suggestion; no match is a per-run user choice (literal value or mint a new token). Three outcomes when a tagged `componentRef` is hit: 1. **Resolves in the open file** — bind/instantiate. Happy path. 2. **Known key, missing from this file** — surface to the user rather than guess. Also how a stale symbol-table export gets caught — the Writer always re-verifies live, so staleness only ever bites the agent's tags, never the write itself. 3. **Explicitly flagged as new** — Writer creates the component from the translated subtree, registers it in the file, and **writes the fresh key back into the symbol table**. The mirror runs both directions: Figma → code by scrape, code → Figma → code by this write-back. ### Fuzzy-match confirmation is a user setting, not a hardcoded default Whether an *exact* match auto-binds silently or still surfaces for confirmation is a plugin setting, not fixed behavior: - **Default: auto-bind exact matches silently.** An exact match to a Variable/Style has no ambiguity — it gets the same no-friction treatment as tagged key-resolution. The confirmation list is reserved for close-but-not-exact matches, where there's a genuine judgment call. - **Toggle: confirm everything.** Users who want to review every binding before it's written — including exact matches — can flip this on. Slower per-import, but nothing lands without explicit sign-off. This setting lives in plugin `clientStorage` alongside snippet storage (below). --- ## Snippet storage Snippets (captured or generated) are stored in the Figma plugin's `clientStorage` only — no sync service, no account system. A snippet made on one machine is not insertable from another machine. This matches the rest of the system's file-hand-off philosophy: nothing here depends on a hosted backend, and it keeps the plugin's only state surface local and simple. Revisit if cross-machine reuse becomes a real user request — it would need a hosted service and auth that no other part of this architecture currently needs. --- ## Publish matrix Four workspace packages. Only one of them ever runs `npm publish` — the other three ship somewhere else entirely, or nowhere. | Package | Ships via | Notes | |---|---|---| | `agent-kit` | **npm** | The flagship, installable CLI. Any project can add it and generate Figma files straight from its own code. | | `figma-plugin` | Figma Community | Reviewed and distributed by Figma, not npm. Importer, scraper, Node Writer. Built on `@create-figma-plugin` (see Decisions). | | `extension` | Chrome Web Store | Reviewed and distributed by Google, not npm. Live-page capture only. | | `core` | *private* | Never published, never leaves the monorepo. Exists only for clean cross-bundler imports. | --- ## Repo layout ``` flowfig/ ├── package.json # npm workspaces: "packages/*" ├── tsconfig.base.json ├── packages/ │ ├── core/ # private — resolver, translator, design-system matching │ ├── figma-plugin/ # manifest.json (Figma's) + main/ui + scraper + Node Writer │ ├── extension/ # manifest.json (Chrome MV3) + content script + popup │ └── agent-kit/ # the one npm-published package — CLI + tagging + agent guide ├── examples/ # shared fixtures — keeps the two adapters honest against one contract ├── docs/ # ships to consumers — incl. agent-kit's generated AGENT-GUIDE.md └── Documentation/ # dev-only — architecture rationale, decisions log ``` --- ## CSS resolution — why the two producers differ The extension never needs a cascade engine of its own — it's already running inside a real, rendered tab, so `getComputedStyle()`/`getBoundingClientRect()` give it ground truth for free. It only needs `core/resolver`'s normalization step and `core/format`; it never even bundles `core/translator`, since capture files are translated later, inside the plugin. agent-kit has no browser at all, so it depends on a real one: **Playwright, as a hard dependency from day one** — chosen over a lighter static-parse-first fallback chain for simplicity, and because it's genuinely more capable (true layout for non-auto-layout content and intrinsic sizing that static CSS parsing or jsdom alone can't produce — jsdom resolves cascade but has no layout engine at all). --- ## MVP roadmap | Phase | Focus | Notes | |---|---|---| | P0 | Core engine skeleton | Resolver + Translator as pure, fixture-tested TS packages — no browser or Figma runtime needed to validate the schema. | | P1 | Figma plugin — writer + scraper | Node Writer proven against hand-built fixture files; scraper exports the first real symbol table. Scaffolded on `@create-figma-plugin`. Nothing downstream is useful without this existing first. | | P2 | agent-kit CLI — the flagship | Playwright resolve+translate, tagging convention, published to npm. This is the product most users actually install. | | P3 | Chrome extension | Secondary capture producer, for arbitrary existing pages rather than agent-generated ones. **Scope now includes basic heuristic structural component-matching**, not just value-level token parity (see Decisions — this widens P3 relative to the original cut). | | P4 | Design-system parity depth | Variant-matching precision, fuzzy-match tuning, symbol-table sync UX. Builds on P3's baseline structural matching rather than introducing it. | | P5 | Fidelity polish | Vector-vs-raster handling, font fallback tables, gradients/shadows — effects. Multi-viewport/interactive-state capture is a deliberate boundary, not a queued phase. | --- ## Foreseeable issues — already designed around 1. **Schema/version skew.** agent-kit publishes to npm instantly; figma-plugin goes through Figma Community's review, which can lag days. Every file carries an explicit `formatVersion`; the plugin **hard-rejects** a mismatch outright rather than attempting best-effort migration (see Decisions) — fails loudly and specifically rather than misparsing. 2. **Fonts, twice over.** `figma.loadFontAsync` only works with fonts the file actually has — needs a fallback-mapping table. Separately, Playwright needs the same webfont to lay out text correctly in the first place, which can fail quietly in a network-restricted agent sandbox. 3. **Playwright's footprint in agent sandboxes.** Install/launch friction is a real setup step for agent-kit's actual audience (CI-like, constrained environments) — documented explicitly in the agent guide, not assumed to "just work." 4. **Vector vs. raster.** Inline ``/background photographs → image fill. An explicit detection rule, not an accident of whatever's easiest. 5. **Scope boundary.** One fixed viewport, one DOM state, per generation. Hover/focus/transitions/breakpoints are out of scope for v1 — stated up front, not discovered later. --- ## Decisions 1. **Snippet storage:** plugin-local `clientStorage` only. No sync service. See Snippet storage section above. 2. **Fuzzy-match confirmation:** a user-facing plugin setting, not a fixed default. Ships defaulted to auto-bind-on-exact-match, with a toggle for confirm-everything. See Node Writer section above. 3. **Component-level detection for arbitrary captured pages:** included in v1 (P3), not deferred. The Chrome extension ships with basic heuristic structural component-matching alongside value-level token parity. This widens P3's scope versus the original value-only cut, and P4 now builds on that baseline rather than introducing structural matching itself. 4. **Format-version policy:** hard-reject a `formatVersion` mismatch outright. No best-effort backward migration. Simpler, safer, and consistent with "fails loudly and specifically" in Foreseeable Issues. 5. **Plugin build tooling:** adopt `@create-figma-plugin` for the `figma-plugin` package rather than a hand-rolled build. Saves setup time for P1 and is the widely-used convention for Figma plugin tooling. --- *flowfig · architecture plan · updated 2026-07-18 · decisions resolved, repo scaffolding starting*

input.prompt:
You are helping design the P0 phase of a brand-new, currently-empty monorepo called "flowfig". The repo right now contains only a README.md — there is no existing code, no package.json, no tsconfig. Do not spend time searching for existing patterns; there are none. This is greenfield design work. Read the repo yourself at /workspaces/flowfig to confirm the empty state (just README.md) before designing.

## Product context

flowfig bridges two producers of UI (an existing-page-capturing Chrome extension, and an agent-kit CLI that renders agent-generated code via Playwright) into a single JSON file format, which a Figma plugin's "Node Writer" consumes to create/update real Figma nodes. Full architecture is below — read it carefully, it contains binding decisions:

---
Two producers, one translation bridge, one writer. Existing pages get captured by a Chrome extension. Generated code gets compiled by an installable CLI. Both land on the same file format, and only one module — the Figma plugin's Node Writer — is ever allowed to touch figma.*.

THE THREE FILE KINDS (this is the target schema P0 must define):
| File | Produced by | Contains | Consumed by |
| *.capture.flowfig.json | extension (resolve() only) | Resolved-but-untranslated tree — raw arbitrary CSS | plugin: full translate + fuzzy-match + write |
| *.translated.flowfig.json | agent-kit (resolve() + translate()) | Already-translated node spec, with tagged component/token refs | plugin: key-resolution + write only |
| .flowfig/symbols.json | plugin's scraper | Symbol table — components/variables/styles scraped live | agent-kit + the agent itself, read off disk |

TAGGING CONVENTION (source markup the translator must recognize):
<div data-flowfig-component="a1b2c3" data-flowfig-props='{"size":"lg"}'>  — whole-component reference, resolved by key
<div data-flowfig-new-component="CardHeader">  — explicitly new, writer creates it and writes the key back
.price { background: var(--ffig-color-accent-500); }  — token-level reference, CSS custom property naming convention --ffig-*, survives cascade because the CLI reads declared CSS source, not just computed output

NODE WRITER RESOLUTION (informs what fields the translated schema needs, even though Node Writer itself is built in P1, not P0):
- Key resolution (tagged): deterministic bind/instantiate by key looked up live via Plugin API.
- Fuzzy matching (untagged): exact match binds a Variable/Style silently by default (this is a user-toggleable plugin setting — default auto-bind-exact, toggle to always-confirm); close match surfaces as suggestion; no match is a per-run user choice (literal value or mint new token).
- Tagged componentRef has 3 outcomes: resolves in open file (bind/instantiate); known key but missing from this file (surface to user, don't guess); explicitly flagged new (Writer creates component, writes fresh key back into symbol table — mirror runs both directions).

CSS RESOLUTION — WHY THE TWO PRODUCERS DIFFER (this shapes what the resolver's INPUT contract must look like, since it has to serve both producers uniformly):
The extension runs inside a real rendered tab, so getComputedStyle()/getBoundingClientRect() give it ground truth for free. It only needs core/resolver's normalization step and core/format; it never bundles core/translator, since capture files are translated later, inside the plugin (capture files are resolved but UNtranslated).
agent-kit has no browser, so it depends on Playwright (hard dependency from day one, chosen over jsdom/static-parse because it gives a real layout engine — true layout for non-auto-layout content and intrinsic sizing that jsdom/static CSS parsing can't produce). agent-kit's pipeline is resolve() THEN translate() — both steps, producing an already-translated file.

REPO LAYOUT (already decided, described in the architecture doc — you should follow this shape):
flowfig/
├── package.json            # npm workspaces: "packages/*"
├── tsconfig.base.json
├── packages/
│   ├── core/                # private — resolver, translator, design-system matching. NEVER published, never leaves the monorepo, exists only for clean cross-bundler imports (each downstream package's own bundler — esbuild via @create-figma-plugin for figma-plugin, some CLI bundler for agent-kit, webpack/esbuild for the MV3 extension — needs to consume core cleanly, targeting its OWN runtime, so core should NOT prescribe a single compiled target)
│   ├── figma-plugin/         # P1 — not built yet, stub only for P0
│   ├── extension/            # P3 — not built yet, stub only for P0
│   └── agent-kit/            # P2 — not built yet, stub only for P0
├── examples/                 # shared fixtures — keeps the two adapters honest against one contract (both extension and agent-kit resolver output must satisfy the same fixture-tested contract)
├── docs/                     # ships to consumers, incl. agent-kit's generated AGENT-GUIDE.md (populated in P2, placeholder for now)
└── Documentation/            # dev-only, already contains architecture-plan.md

MVP ROADMAP — P0 IS THE ONLY PHASE IN SCOPE RIGHT NOW:
P0 | Core engine skeleton | Resolver + Translator as pure, fixture-tested TS packages — no browser or Figma runtime needed to validate the schema.
(P1 figma-plugin, P2 agent-kit, P3 extension, P4 parity depth, P5 fidelity polish all come LATER — do not design their internals, just leave clean seams for them.)

Already-decided tooling/process constraints (do not re-litigate these, they are final):
- npm workspaces (not pnpm/yarn)
- formatVersion field on every file, hard-reject on mismatch (no migration shims)
- figma-plugin will use @create-figma-plugin later (P1) — irrelevant to P0 but means core's build output must be consumable by esbuild-based bundling eventually
- Snippet storage, fuzzy-match-setting, capture-scope-includes-structural-matching are settled decisions for LATER phases (P1/P3), not relevant to P0's job

## What you need to design (P0 only)

1. **Monorepo scaffold**: root package.json (workspaces config, scripts), tsconfig.base.json (strict mode, target/module choices that work for a package meant to be consumed by three very different downstream bundlers — Figma plugin sandbox, Node CLI, browser extension), test runner choice (recommend one: Vitest vs Jest, justify briefly), and whether core ships as compiled dist+d.ts or is consumed as raw TS source via workspace protocol by every downstream bundler (recommend one, justify against the "clean cross-bundler imports" requirement above).

2. **packages/core internal structure**: concrete src/ layout for format/, resolver/, translator/ modules. For each, define:
   - format/: the actual TypeScript types for FlowfigNode (recursive tree — needs at minimum: node type discriminant like frame/text/vector/image, geometry/box, layout-mode e.g. autoLayout mapping from flex/grid, a style/props bag, text content+runs for text nodes, children), ComponentRef, TokenRef, the CaptureFile vs TranslatedFile file-level wrapper shape (formatVersion + root + metadata), and formatVersion constant/validation.
   - resolver/: the pure function(s) that normalize a generic "computed style + geometry + DOM structure" snapshot (something BOTH getComputedStyle-in-a-real-tab AND Playwright's page.evaluate can produce in the same shape) into a resolved FlowfigNode tree. Define the resolver's INPUT type precisely — this is the contract both producers must satisfy.
   - translator/: the pure function(s) that take a resolved tree + the data-flowfig-* tag info (design how tags survive from source DOM into the resolver's input/output so translator can read them) + a symbol-table shape (stub/mock shape is fine, real scraper is P1) and produce a TranslatedFile with componentRef/newComponent annotations and CSS custom-property token refs (--ffig-* convention) swapped in for literal style values.

3. **examples/ fixtures approach**: what a fixture looks like (e.g. paired input DOM/computed-style snapshot + expected resolved JSON + expected translated JSON) and how core's tests consume them, such that figma-plugin/agent-kit/extension can later reuse the SAME fixtures to stay honest against the one contract.

4. **Concrete file list** for P0: every file to create, in order, with a one-line purpose each. Include root config files, packages/core/*, packages/{figma-plugin,extension,agent-kit}/package.json stubs (just enough to be valid npm workspace members, no real code), examples/ fixture files, and a docs/ placeholder.

5. **Verification plan**: how to prove P0 works end to end — what test command to run, what it should assert (fixture-in → resolved-out and resolved-in → translated-out matching expected JSON), and any typecheck/build command.

Do NOT design P1-P5 internals. Do NOT pick a Playwright integration yet (that's P2). Focus entirely on making core/format, core/resolver, core/translator real, tested, and provably correct against fixtures — that's what "no browser or Figma runtime needed to validate the schema" means.

Return a detailed, concrete implementation plan: file-by-file, with key type definitions written out in full (not hand-waved), a recommended test framework and monorepo build strategy with justification, and the verification command(s) to run.

# flowfig — P0 scaffolding: repo folder structure

## Context

The architecture plan (`Documentation/architecture-plan.md`) is refined and its open decisions are resolved. The repo currently contains only `README.md`. Before writing any real engine code (resolver/translator schema — that's the next, separate step), the user wants the repo laid out to match the documented structure: workspaces wired up, every package present as a stub, so the shape is right before content gets filled in phase by phase (P0 → P5).

This step is scaffolding only: create directories and the minimal files needed for `npm install` to recognize all workspace members. No resolver/translator/format logic yet.

## Structure to create

```
flowfig/
├── package.json            # root — npm workspaces: "packages/*"
├── tsconfig.base.json      # shared strict TS config, extended by each package
├── .gitignore              # node_modules, dist, etc.
├── packages/
│   ├── core/                # private, never published — resolver/translator/format live here (P0, next step)
│   │   └── package.json
│   ├── figma-plugin/         # P1 — Node Writer, scraper, importer (built later)
│   │   └── package.json
│   ├── extension/            # P3 — Chrome MV3 capture (built later)
│   │   └── package.json
│   └── agent-kit/            # P2 — npm-published CLI (built later)
│       └── package.json
├── examples/                 # shared fixtures directory (populated once core exists)
├── docs/                     # ships to consumers, incl. agent-kit's future AGENT-GUIDE.md
└── Documentation/            # already exists — architecture-plan.md lives here
```

## Details per file

- **Root `package.json`**: `"private": true`, `"workspaces": ["packages/*"]`, name `flowfig`, no dependencies yet — just the workspace declaration and placeholder `scripts` (`build`, `test`, `typecheck`) that no-op or `echo` until real packages populate them.
- **Root `tsconfig.base.json`**: strict mode on, no target/module opinion baked in yet beyond sane defaults (`ES2022`/`NodeNext`) — each package's own `tsconfig.json` will extend this and override target per its runtime (Figma sandbox vs Node CLI vs browser extension) when that package is actually built.
- **`.gitignore`**: standard Node ignores (`node_modules`, `dist`, `.DS_Store`, etc.).
- **Each `packages/*/package.json`**: minimal valid npm workspace member — `name` (e.g. `@flowfig/core`, `@flowfig/figma-plugin`, `@flowfig/extension`, `@flowfig/agent-kit`), `version": "0.0.0"`, `"private": true` (except `agent-kit`, which will eventually publish — still fine as private for now until P2 flips it), no dependencies or scripts yet. No `src/` files inside these packages yet — that's each package's own phase.
- **`examples/`**: created as an empty directory (with a `.gitkeep` or a one-line `README.md` noting its purpose) since it has no content until core's schema exists.
- **`docs/`**: same — empty placeholder directory with a short `README.md` noting `AGENT-GUIDE.md` lands here in P2.
- **`Documentation/`**: already exists with `architecture-plan.md`; no change needed here.

## What this deliberately does NOT do

- No resolver/translator/format TypeScript code (core's actual content — next step, its own plan).
- No `@create-figma-plugin` scaffold inside `figma-plugin/` yet (P1).
- No Playwright setup inside `agent-kit/` yet (P2).
- No MV3 manifest inside `extension/` yet (P3).
- No test framework wired in yet — that arrives with core's first real code, since there's nothing to test until then.

## Verification

- `npm install` from repo root succeeds and recognizes all four workspace packages (`npm ls --workspaces` lists `@flowfig/core`, `@flowfig/figma-plugin`, `@flowfig/extension`, `@flowfig/agent-kit`).
- `git status` shows the new directories/files as untracked, nothing else touched.

input.description: Architect core package
input.prompt:
You are designing the architecture for the `core` package of a monorepo called "flowfig", at /workspaces/flowfig/packages/core. This is a greenfield package — it currently contains only a placeholder package.json. Do not write any TypeScript source code yet — you are writing an architecture/planning document only.

First, read /workspaces/flowfig/Documentation/architecture-plan.md in full for the complete product context (pipeline, file kinds, tagging convention, Node Writer resolution modes, publish matrix, roadmap phases, resolved decisions). Also read /workspaces/flowfig/README.md and check /workspaces/flowfig/packages/core/package.json.

## What `core` is

`core` is the ONLY package never published — it's a private shared library consumed by the other three packages (figma-plugin, extension, agent-kit), each via its own bundler. It owns:
1. **format** — the flowfig JSON schema types (the three file kinds: `*.capture.flowfig.json`, `*.translated.flowfig.json`, `.flowfig/symbols.json`), formatVersion, and validation.
2. **resolver** — pure normalization: takes a generic "computed style + geometry + DOM structure" snapshot (something BOTH a live browser tab's getComputedStyle()/getBoundingClientRect() AND Playwright's page.evaluate() can produce in the same shape) and produces a resolved node tree. This is the shared contract both producers (Chrome extension, agent-kit) must satisfy — it's what keeps the two adapters honest against one contract per `examples/`.
3. **translator** — pure transform: takes a resolved tree + the source markup's `data-flowfig-*` tag attributes + a symbol-table shape, and produces a translated tree with componentRef/newComponent annotations and `--ffig-*` CSS custom property token refs swapped in for literal style values. NOTE: only agent-kit calls translator directly (capture files are translated later, inside the plugin) — so translator must be usable both by agent-kit's CLI pipeline AND later by the figma-plugin package's Import step.
4. **design-system matching** — the fuzzy-matching logic shared by both plugin and (per the resolved decision) the extension's v1 structural component-matching.

Key resolved decisions that constrain this design (do not re-litigate):
- npm workspaces, TS strict mode.
- `core` ships as raw TS consumed directly via the workspace by each downstream bundler — NOT a separately compiled dist, because each consumer targets a different runtime (Figma plugin sandbox, Node CLI via agent-kit, browser extension MV3) and needs to compile it with its own bundler/target. Confirm this reasoning holds and flag if you find a strong reason to reconsider.
- Every file carries an explicit `formatVersion`; mismatches are hard-rejected, no migration shims.
- Tagging convention is exactly: `data-flowfig-component="<key>"` + optional `data-flowfig-props='{"...json..."}'`, `data-flowfig-new-component="<Name>"`, and CSS custom properties named `--ffig-*` as token refs.
- The extension's v1 now includes basic heuristic structural component-matching (not just value-level token parity) — so `core`'s design-system-matching module needs to serve both a "just match tokens/values" mode and a "also attempt structural component matching" mode.

## Research required (use WebSearch/WebFetch — this is real research, not guesswork)

1. How does Figma's Plugin API represent nodes, auto-layout, variables, and component instances? (Search: "figma plugin api node types", "figma plugin api auto layout properties", "figma plugin api variables API", "figma component instance API") — you need enough understanding to design a resolved-node schema that the Node Writer (built later, in figma-plugin) can actually map onto Figma nodes without redesigning the schema later.
2. How do getComputedStyle() and Playwright's evaluate-based style extraction differ in practice (units, shorthand expansion, pseudo-elements, inherited values)? This affects what "normalization" the resolver must do to produce ONE consistent shape from two different capture mechanisms.
3. Common approaches for CSS flexbox/grid → Figma auto-layout mapping (search for prior art: html-to-figma tools, figma-html plugins, similar OSS projects) — learn from what already exists rather than inventing from scratch.
4. How design-token-matching / fuzzy color-and-spacing matching is typically implemented (nearest-neighbor on token values, Delta-E for color distance, etc.) if you find good references.

## Deliverable

Write a file at /workspaces/flowfig/packages/core/ARCHITECTURE.md with these sections:
- **Purpose & Scope** (2-3 sentences, what this package is and is not)
- **Requirements** — functional and non-functional, derived from the product context above
- **Research Findings** — what you learned from the research above, with enough specificity to justify design choices (cite what you searched, briefly)
- **Proposed Architecture** — concrete src/ module layout (format/, resolver/, translator/, matching/), and for each module: its key exported TypeScript types/interfaces written out in full (not hand-waved — actual type definitions), and its main function signatures
- **The Resolver Input Contract** — the precise shape both the Chrome extension and agent-kit/Playwright must produce, since this is the linchpin contract of the whole system
- **Fixture/Testing Strategy** — how `examples/` fixtures pair with this package's tests to prove resolver and translator correctness without a browser or Figma runtime
- **Phase Task Breakdown** — concrete ordered tasks to build this package for P0 (per the roadmap, P0 = "Resolver + Translator as pure, fixture-tested TS packages")
- **Open Risks / Questions** — anything genuinely uncertain that a human should weigh in on

Be concrete and opinionated — this document should be usable directly as an implementation plan, not a vague survey. Write actual TypeScript interface definitions in code blocks. When you're done, report back a brief summary (under 200 words) of what you wrote and any risks you flagged.

input.description: Architect figma-plugin package
input.prompt:
You are designing the architecture for the `figma-plugin` package of a monorepo called "flowfig", at /workspaces/flowfig/packages/figma-plugin. This is a greenfield package — it currently contains only a placeholder package.json. Do not write any TypeScript source code yet — you are writing an architecture/planning document only.

First, read /workspaces/flowfig/Documentation/architecture-plan.md in full for the complete product context (pipeline, file kinds, tagging convention, Node Writer resolution modes, publish matrix, roadmap phases, resolved decisions). Also read /workspaces/flowfig/README.md and check /workspaces/flowfig/packages/figma-plugin/package.json.

## What `figma-plugin` is

This is the ONLY module in the entire system allowed to call `figma.*` — everything else produces files, this is what reads them and writes real Figma nodes. It ships via Figma Community (not npm). It has three responsibilities:
1. **Import** — accepts either `*.capture.flowfig.json` (untranslated — needs full translate + fuzzy-match) or `*.translated.flowfig.json` (already tagged — needs only key-resolution) and writes real nodes.
2. **Scrape** — walks the currently-open Figma file's components/variables/styles and exports `.flowfig/symbols.json`, the symbol table that agent-kit and the agent itself read back for tagging. This mirror runs in both directions (scrape = Figma → code, write-back of newly-created component keys = code → Figma → code).
3. **Node Writer** — the actual `figma.*`-calling logic with two resolution modes:
   - **Key resolution** (tagged): deterministic — look up the key live via the Plugin API, bind/instantiate. Three outcomes: resolves in open file (happy path); known key but missing from this file (surface to user, don't guess — this is also how the Writer catches a stale symbol-table export, since it always re-verifies live); explicitly flagged new (`data-flowfig-new-component`) — Writer creates the component from the translated subtree, registers it, and writes the fresh key back into the symbol table.
   - **Fuzzy matching** (untagged): exact match auto-binds a Variable/Style; close match surfaces as suggestion; no match is a per-run user choice (literal value or mint new token). Whether EXACT matches auto-bind silently or always surface for confirmation is a **user-facing plugin setting** (default: auto-bind exact silently, toggle: confirm everything) — this setting lives in plugin `clientStorage`.

Resolved decisions that constrain this design (do not re-litigate):
- Built on **`@create-figma-plugin`** framework (not hand-rolled build) — you should research its actual conventions (project structure, build tooling, UI framework it uses) since this package's scaffold must follow them.
- Snippet storage (captured/generated snippets) is **plugin-local `clientStorage` only** — no sync service, no account system, no backend.
- `formatVersion` mismatches are **hard-rejected** outright — no best-effort migration.
- This package is P1 in the roadmap — the roadmap explicitly says "Nothing downstream is useful without this existing first," i.e., figma-plugin's Node Writer + scraper are the critical path before agent-kit (P2) or the extension (P3) are worth building.
- Consumes `@flowfig/core`'s format/resolver/translator/matching modules (a sibling package in the same monorepo, currently being architected in parallel — assume it exposes: file-format types for capture/translated files and the symbol table, a `translate()` function, and a design-system fuzzy-matching module. Do not invent conflicting duplicate logic — this package's job is to CALL core's translate/match functions and do the actual figma.* writing, not reimplement resolution logic).

## Research required (use WebSearch/WebFetch — this is real research, not guesswork)

1. `@create-figma-plugin` — actual project structure, config format, build commands, UI toolkit it ships (search: "create-figma-plugin github", "create-figma-plugin manifest config ui"). You need to know its real conventions to plan a scaffold that will actually work.
2. Figma Plugin API capabilities and hard constraints relevant to this design: `clientStorage` API and its size/quota limits, `figma.variables` API (creating/binding Variables), `figma.createComponent`/component API, `figma.loadFontAsync` behavior and constraints, network access restrictions inside the plugin sandbox (can plugins fetch external resources at all, and under what manifest permissions?), and how a plugin reads a file from disk (since it needs to import `*.flowfig.json` files — search how existing plugins handle file import, e.g. via `<input type=file>` in the UI iframe vs `figma.ui.postMessage`).
3. How existing plugins (e.g. Tokens Studio for Figma, or other variable/import plugins) structure symbol-table-like exports and import UX, for prior art — search for real examples, don't invent from a vacuum.
4. Figma's plugin review process for Figma Community (timelines, common rejection reasons) — this matters because the architecture doc calls out review lag as a foreseeable schema-skew risk.

## Deliverable

Write a file at /workspaces/flowfig/packages/figma-plugin/ARCHITECTURE.md with these sections:
- **Purpose & Scope**
- **Requirements** — functional and non-functional
- **Research Findings** — cite what you searched and what you learned, with enough specificity to justify design choices, especially real API constraints (quotas, permissions, sandboxing) that shape the design
- **Proposed Architecture** — concrete file/module layout following `@create-figma-plugin` conventions (main thread code vs UI iframe code split, message-passing shape between them), and the Node Writer's internal structure (how it dispatches between key-resolution and fuzzy-matching, how it calls into `@flowfig/core`)
- **clientStorage Schema** — the actual shape of what's stored (plugin settings incl. the fuzzy-match-confirmation toggle, snippets)
- **Import UX Flow** — step by step, how a user gets a `*.flowfig.json` file into the plugin and what happens on each of the three tagged-componentRef outcomes
- **Symbol Table Scraper Design** — what it walks, what `.flowfig/symbols.json` looks like concretely (write the actual JSON shape)
- **Phase Task Breakdown** — ordered tasks to build this package for P1
- **Open Risks / Questions**

Be concrete and opinionated — this should be directly usable as an implementation plan. When done, report back a brief summary (under 200 words) of what you wrote and any risks you flagged.

input.description: Architect extension package
input.prompt:
You are designing the architecture for the `extension` package of a monorepo called "flowfig", at /workspaces/flowfig/packages/extension. This is a greenfield package — it currently contains only a placeholder package.json. Do not write any TypeScript source code yet — you are writing an architecture/planning document only.

First, read /workspaces/flowfig/Documentation/architecture-plan.md in full for the complete product context (pipeline, file kinds, tagging convention, publish matrix, roadmap phases, resolved decisions). Also read /workspaces/flowfig/README.md and check /workspaces/flowfig/packages/extension/package.json.

## What `extension` is

A Chrome extension (Manifest V3), shipped via the Chrome Web Store (not npm). It is Producer A: it captures ARBITRARY EXISTING live web pages (not agent-generated code) and turns them into `*.capture.flowfig.json` files. Its defining advantage: it runs inside a real, already-rendered tab, so it gets `getComputedStyle()`/`getBoundingClientRect()` ground truth for free — it never needs a cascade/layout engine of its own. It only needs `@flowfig/core`'s resolver (for normalization) and format module — it does NOT bundle `@flowfig/core`'s translator, because capture files are resolved-but-UNtranslated; translation happens later, inside the figma-plugin package, when the file is imported.

A resolved decision (do not re-litigate) that WIDENS this package's original planned scope: v1 of the capture path now includes **basic heuristic structural component-matching**, not just value-level token parity (colors/spacing/type bound to tokens). This means the extension (or the resolver it calls into) needs to make a first-pass attempt at recognizing repeated structural patterns (e.g., "this cluster of elements looks like a repeated card/button pattern") in addition to binding individual CSS values to design tokens. Deeper precision tuning of this is deferred to a later phase (P4), but the v1 baseline must exist here in P3.

Other resolved/fixed context:
- Ships via Chrome Web Store, MV3 only (not MV2).
- `formatVersion` is hard-rejected on mismatch — the extension must stamp the current formatVersion into every capture file it produces.
- This is P3 in the roadmap — it depends on `@flowfig/core` (P0) and the figma-plugin's importer (P1) already existing; agent-kit (P2) is a sibling producer, not a dependency.
- Consumes `@flowfig/core` (a sibling package in this monorepo, currently being architected in parallel — assume it exposes a resolver `resolve()` function taking a generic "computed style + geometry + DOM structure" snapshot, format types for the capture file, and a design-system fuzzy/structural-matching module the extension can call for the v1 structural-matching requirement above). Do NOT invent conflicting duplicate resolution logic — this package's job is to gather the DOM/style snapshot from a REAL page and hand it to core's resolver, plus drive the popup/UI and file-save flow.

## Research required (use WebSearch/WebFetch — this is real research, not guesswork)

1. Chrome MV3 architecture specifics relevant here: content scripts vs the extension service worker vs the popup — what each can and can't do, message-passing patterns between them (search: "chrome extension manifest v3 content script service worker messaging"). You need this to decide where DOM-walking + getComputedStyle/getBoundingClientRect actually runs (content script, almost certainly) vs where file-save/UI happens (popup or a dedicated tab).
2. How to reliably walk getComputedStyle() across an entire page including shadow DOM and iframes (search: "getComputedStyle shadow dom traversal", "chrome extension cross-origin iframe access limitations") — this is a real, documented pain point; find out what MV3 extensions can and cannot reach.
3. How MV3 extensions save files to disk from a content script/popup context (search: "chrome extension manifest v3 downloads API save file", "chrome.downloads API") — since the extension must produce a `*.capture.flowfig.json` file the user can hand to the figma-plugin.
4. Prior art for DOM-to-design-tool capture extensions (e.g. html.to.design, Figma's own "Figma to Code"-adjacent capture tools, or similar) for structural-pattern detection heuristics (repeated sibling structure, common class-name clustering) — learn from what exists rather than inventing blind.
5. Chrome Web Store review process (timelines, common rejection reasons, permission-justification requirements) — relevant since broad DOM/host permissions on every page tend to draw review scrutiny.

## Deliverable

Write a file at /workspaces/flowfig/packages/extension/ARCHITECTURE.md with these sections:
- **Purpose & Scope**
- **Requirements** — functional and non-functional
- **Research Findings** — cite what you searched and what you learned, with enough specificity to justify design choices (especially MV3 constraints and shadow-DOM/iframe limitations)
- **Proposed Architecture** — concrete file/module layout (manifest.json shape, content script responsibilities, service worker responsibilities, popup UI responsibilities), and the message-passing flow between them
- **DOM Snapshot Contract** — the precise shape the content script hands to `@flowfig/core`'s resolver (must be compatible with what agent-kit/Playwright will also produce — describe how you're keeping it aligned even though you can't see core's final types yet)
- **Structural Matching v1 Approach** — concretely, what heuristic(s) this package's capture flow will apply for the newly-widened v1 structural-component-matching requirement, and what it deliberately leaves for P4
- **Permissions & Manifest** — what `host_permissions`/`permissions` are actually needed and why, since minimal permissions matter for both user trust and Chrome Web Store review
- **Phase Task Breakdown** — ordered tasks to build this package for P3
- **Open Risks / Questions** — flag anything about shadow DOM, cross-origin iframes, or Chrome Web Store review that could block or reshape this

Be concrete and opinionated — this should be directly usable as an implementation plan. When done, report back a brief summary (under 200 words) of what you wrote and any risks you flagged.

input.description: Architect agent-kit package
input.prompt:
You are designing the architecture for the `agent-kit` package of a monorepo called "flowfig", at /workspaces/flowfig/packages/agent-kit. This is a greenfield package — it currently contains only a placeholder package.json. Do not write any TypeScript source code yet — you are writing an architecture/planning document only.

First, read /workspaces/flowfig/Documentation/architecture-plan.md in full for the complete product context (pipeline, file kinds, tagging convention, publish matrix, roadmap phases, resolved decisions). Also read /workspaces/flowfig/README.md and check /workspaces/flowfig/packages/agent-kit/package.json.

## What `agent-kit` is

This is the FLAGSHIP package — the only one published to npm — an installable CLI that any project can add to generate Figma files straight from its own agent-generated code. It is Producer B: unlike the Chrome extension (which captures arbitrary EXISTING pages), agent-kit targets code that an AI coding agent just generated, tags with the flowfig convention, and wants pushed into Figma. It has no browser of its own, so it depends on a real one: **Playwright, as a hard dependency from day one** (not a lighter static-parse-first fallback chain) — chosen because it gives a true layout engine (real layout for non-auto-layout content and intrinsic sizing) that static CSS parsing or jsdom cannot produce (jsdom resolves cascade but has no layout engine at all).

agent-kit's pipeline is **resolve() THEN translate()** — both steps — producing an already-`*.translated.flowfig.json` file with tagged component/token refs already resolved. It reads `.flowfig/symbols.json` (produced by the figma-plugin's scraper) to resolve tags to real Figma keys before writing the translated file, and it also owns generating **`docs/AGENT-GUIDE.md`** — a generated guide that teaches the coding agent (the one generating the tagged markup in the first place) the local tagging conventions (`data-flowfig-component`, `data-flowfig-new-component`, `data-flowfig-props`, `--ffig-*` custom properties) so it knows how to tag code correctly.

Resolved/fixed context (do not re-litigate):
- Playwright is a hard dependency, not optional — this is final.
- `formatVersion` mismatches are hard-rejected — agent-kit must stamp the current version into every file it writes.
- Foreseeable issue already flagged in the architecture doc: **Playwright's footprint in agent sandboxes** — install/launch friction is a real setup step for agent-kit's actual audience (CI-like, constrained environments), and this must be "documented explicitly in the agent guide, not assumed to just work." Also: fonts are a double problem — `figma.loadFontAsync` only works with fonts the Figma file actually has (needs a fallback-mapping table, but that's more the plugin's problem), while separately Playwright needs the same webfont to lay out text correctly in the first place, which can fail quietly in a network-restricted agent sandbox — agent-kit must handle/detect this.
- This is P2 in the roadmap, after figma-plugin (P1) exists (agent-kit needs a real symbol table format and a plugin that can consume its translated files) and after `@flowfig/core` (P0).
- Consumes `@flowfig/core` (a sibling package in this monorepo, currently being architected in parallel — assume it exposes a `resolve()` function taking a generic "computed style + geometry + DOM structure" snapshot, a `translate()` function taking a resolved tree + tag info + symbol table and producing a translated file, and format types for all three file kinds). Do NOT invent conflicting duplicate resolution/translation logic — this package's job is to drive Playwright to PRODUCE that snapshot, call into core's resolve/translate, handle the CLI/config/npm-package concerns, and generate the agent guide.

## Research required (use WebSearch/WebFetch — this is real research, not guesswork)

1. Playwright's API for extracting computed styles + layout geometry + DOM structure from a rendered page (search: "playwright evaluate getComputedStyle getBoundingClientRect", "playwright page.evaluate DOM serialization") — you need to know what's actually available so the snapshot agent-kit produces can match the SAME shape the Chrome extension's content script produces (both must satisfy `@flowfig/core`'s resolver input contract).
2. Playwright installation/footprint concerns in CI/sandbox/agent environments specifically (search: "playwright ci docker headless install size", "playwright sandboxed environment browser download issues", "playwright in AI agent sandbox no network") — this directly informs the "documented explicitly in the agent guide" requirement above; find out what actually goes wrong (browser binary download blocked, missing system deps, headless flags, etc.) so the guide can be genuinely useful rather than generic.
3. Common CLI framework choices for a Node.js TypeScript CLI meant to `npm install` into arbitrary projects (search: "node cli framework typescript commander vs yargs vs oclif 2025/2026") and common patterns for CLI config files (e.g. `flowfig.config.ts`/`.json`) in similar dev-tool CLIs.
4. How other "generate design tool file from code" tools structure their agent-facing documentation (search for prior art on AI-agent-facing tool guides/llms.txt conventions, e.g. "AGENTS.md convention", "llms.txt convention") since `AGENT-GUIDE.md` is explicitly agent-facing, not human-facing — the writing conventions differ.
5. Playwright font-loading behavior — how to detect a missing/unavailable webfont during rendering so agent-kit can warn rather than silently mis-measuring text (search: "playwright wait for fonts loaded", "document.fonts.ready playwright").

## Deliverable

Write a file at /workspaces/flowfig/packages/agent-kit/ARCHITECTURE.md with these sections:
- **Purpose & Scope**
- **Requirements** — functional and non-functional
- **Research Findings** — cite what you searched and what you learned, with enough specificity to justify design choices (especially Playwright sandbox/CI footprint and font-loading findings)
- **Proposed Architecture** — concrete file/module layout (CLI entry/commands, Playwright driver module, config loading, the resolve+translate pipeline glue into `@flowfig/core`, AGENT-GUIDE.md generator)
- **Playwright Snapshot Contract** — the precise shape this package extracts from a rendered page, and how you're keeping it aligned with the shape the Chrome extension's content script must also produce (you can't see core's or extension's final types yet — describe the coordination approach)
- **CLI Design** — concrete commands/flags/config file shape (write it out)
- **AGENT-GUIDE.md Content Plan** — what sections it needs and why, given it's written FOR a coding agent, not a human developer
- **Sandbox/Footprint Guidance** — concrete, specific troubleshooting content this package must ship (not generic "install playwright") based on your research findings on real failure modes
- **Phase Task Breakdown** — ordered tasks to build this package for P2
- **Open Risks / Questions**

Be concrete and opinionated — this should be directly usable as an implementation plan. When done, report back a brief summary (under 200 words) of what you wrote and any risks you flagged.

lastPrompt: Study the entent of this project and understand requirements of each part and do your own research and then come up with proper plans and architectures for each one and store them and separate file an…

content.text:
The document is written to `/workspaces/flowfig/packages/figma-plugin/ARCHITECTURE.md`.

**Summary of what was written:**

Read the root architecture plan, README, and package.json first, then researched (via web search/fetch): `@create-figma-plugin`'s real conventions (package.json-driven config, generated manifest.json, esbuild + Preact UI, multi-command `menu` support), Figma Plugin API constraints (`clientStorage` 5MB quota scoped per-plugin-per-user, `networkAccess` manifest field, main-thread-vs-UI-iframe file I/O split, `figma.variables`/`createComponentFromNode`/`loadFontAsync` behaviors and their restrictions, `importComponentByKeyAsync` vs local lookup for key resolution), Tokens Studio as prior art for JSON-as-interchange-format design, and Figma Community's review process (documented 5-10 business days but real-world reports of 1-1.5+ month waits, plus concrete rejection reasons).

The doc covers: purpose/scope, functional/non-functional requirements, cited research findings, a concrete file layout (main/ui/shared split, three menu commands: Import/Scrape/Settings), the Node Writer's two-phase plan/commit design with dispatch between key-resolution and fuzzy-matching, a typed message-passing contract, a concrete `clientStorage` schema (per-snippet keys to avoid quota read-modify-write issues), a step-by-step import UX flow covering all six resolution outcomes, a concrete `.flowfig/symbols.json` JSON shape, and a 13-task ordered build plan.

**Key risks flagged:** review-latency-induced schema skew is empirically real (not theoretical); write-back timing after mid-import component creation is unresolved (leaning toward in-memory patch + prompt-to-rescrape, since the main thread can't write disk directly anyway); whether to declare the `teamlibrary` permission in P1 despite no P1 exercise path is a genuine open tradeoff; font-fallback table's data source is undefined; and `@flowfig/core`'s exact API surface is assumed pending its parallel architecture work.
